serving.natural_language_portion: One standard restaurant serving of kimchi chicken fried rice is approximately 350 g, typically served as a single-plate meal.

nutrient_source.title: Fried Rice, with Meat — USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy / NCC Food and Nutrient Database composite, adapted for kimchi chicken fried rice
nutrient_source.organization: USDA Agricultural Research Service — FoodData Central; values cross-referenced with Korean Food Composition Table (National Institute of Agricultural Sciences, Korea)
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No exact entry for kimchi chicken fried rice exists in HPB Singapore Foodo or USDA FoodData Central. Values are estimated from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy entry for 'Rice, fried, with meat' (FDC ID 169715) and adjusted for kimchi contribution (additional sodium, slight increase in dietary fibre, minor carbohydrate reduction) and chicken protein content. Korean Food Composition Table (NIAS Korea) used to cross-check kimchi nutrient contribution. This is a composite estimation; confidence is medium.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2021 — Atkinson FS et al., American Journal of Clinical Nutrition; white rice-based fried rice analogous estimate
gi_source.organization: American Journal of Clinical Nutrition (peer-reviewed journal)
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for kimchi chicken fried rice. GI of 56 is estimated from the closest analogous food: fried rice (white rice base, GI ~56–65 range per Atkinson et al. 2021 international GI tables). The lower end of 56 is applied to account for the acidic kimchi component, which is known to attenuate postprandial glycemic response (vinegar/lactic acid effect documented in peer-reviewed literature). Glycemic load calculated as (GI × carbohydrate_g per serving) / 100 = (56 × 69.3) / 100 ≈ 38.8 for a 350 g serving (carbohydrate_g per serving = 19.8 × 3.5 = 69.3 g).

hypertension.key_factor: High sodium content from kimchi, soy sauce, and seasoning
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should limit portion size to half a standard serving and request reduced soy sauce and kimchi to keep sodium intake below 600 mg per meal; estimated sodium per full 350 g serving is approximately 1680 mg, which approaches or exceeds the recommended daily limit for hypertensive patients.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Medium glycemic index white rice base with moderate carbohydrate load per serving
type2_diabetes.guidance: Individuals with Type 2 Diabetes should be aware that a standard 350 g serving delivers approximately 69 g of carbohydrate with a glycemic load of ~39, which is high; portion reduction to 150–175 g and pairing with additional non-starchy vegetables can help moderate postprandial blood glucose, while the lactic acid in kimchi may provide a modest glycemic-attenuating effect.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Elevated sodium and moderate saturated fat from cooking oil and chicken skin
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Individuals with cardiovascular disease should choose skinless chicken breast, minimise added oil, and limit portion to reduce sodium and saturated fat intake; the dish provides approximately 3.85 g saturated fat per 350 g serving, and the high sodium content warrants caution in patients with heart failure or hypertension-related cardiovascular risk.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Kimchi Chicken Fried Rice (김치 닭고기 볶음밥; Chinese: 泡菜鸡肉炒饭) is a Korean-origin stir-fried rice dish prepared by wok-frying cooked white rice with fermented napa cabbage kimchi, diced or sliced chicken (breast or thigh), garlic, spring onion, sesame oil, and soy sauce. The dish is characterised by a bold, tangy, and mildly spicy flavour profile derived from the fermented kimchi, with a savoury umami depth from soy sauce and sesame oil. The texture is slightly chewy from the rice grains, with tender chicken pieces and soft kimchi throughout. In Singapore, this dish is commonly served at Korean restaurants and Korean-fusion casual dining outlets as a single-plate meal. Nutritionally, a standard 350 g serving provides approximately 518 kcal, 25 g protein, 16 g fat, and 69 g carbohydrate. The dish is notably high in sodium (~1680 mg per serving) due to kimchi and soy sauce, making it a concern for individuals managing hypertension or cardiovascular disease. The medium glycemic index (~56) and high glycemic load per serving (~39) require portion control for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes. The fermented kimchi component contributes probiotics, dietary fibre, and vitamin C, adding nutritional value beyond the rice base. The dish is not suitable for vegetarians or vegans due to chicken content, and contains gluten from soy sauce and sesame allergens.

confidence_reason: No exact entry for kimchi chicken fried rice was found in HPB Singapore Foodo, USDA FoodData Central, or the Korean Food Composition Table as a composite dish. Nutrient values are estimated from USDA SR Legacy fried rice with meat (FDC ID 169715) adjusted for kimchi and chicken contributions, cross-referenced with NIAS Korea data. GI is an analogous estimate from peer-reviewed international GI tables. Confidence is medium due to the composite estimation approach.
notes: Cuisine corrected from 'Japanese' (input error) to 'Korean' based on dish identity, restaurant name ('Korean Cuisine - Funan Mall'), and cuisine tags. Food group corrected from 'Meat and alternatives' to 'Grains and staples' as rice is the primary macronutrient base. Vitamin D is -1 as it is not reported in the USDA SR Legacy source for this dish category. Vitamin E is -1 as it is not reported in the primary source. Selenium is -1 as it is not reported in the primary source. Trans fat is 0.0 confirmed as negligible in this dish type per USDA data. Halal status set to false as kimchi may contain non-halal seafood ingredients (fish sauce, shrimp paste) depending on preparation; restaurant-specific verification required. GI of 56 is an analogous estimate; direct measurement for this dish is not available in approved sources.
